title: Ribbon bookmarks sewn into a binding
subtitle: Attaching one or more ribbon markers during assembly
excerpt: A ribbon bookmark attached at the head of the spine before casing in is the most elegant solution to marking a page. Fitted during assembly it stays permanently, lies flat, and never damages the paper. This tutorial covers the standard method for both sewn and adhesive bindings.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on forwarding: mention of ribbon markers attached at the head before lining. The technique is standard in European case-binding tradition.

glossaryTerms.definition: A narrow woven or sewn strip at the head and tail of a book spine, originally structural and now mainly decorative.

content.text: Ribbon bookmarks are fitted at the head of the spine before the spine liner or mull goes on. This is the moment in the binding sequence when the spine is exposed and the adhesive is fresh. The ribbon sits in a groove pressed into the head of the spine and is glued in place, then covered by the spine lining. Once cased in, the ribbon emerges from under the
and trails to the tail of the book.

items.name: Ribbon, 6 to 10 mm wide, grosgrain or silk
items.notes: Cut 30 mm longer than the finished book is tall. Natural-fibre ribbons (silk, cotton grosgrain) age better than polyester.

items.name: PVA, acid-free
items.notes: For gluing the ribbon into the spine head.

items.name: Bone folder
items.notes: For pressing the ribbon into the groove.

items.name: Craft scissors
items.notes: For cutting the ribbon cleanly. Seal the cut end with a drop of PVA or singe it if the ribbon is a woven synthetic.

content.text: For a sewn book, attach the ribbon after the kettle-stitch on the final signature is complete, before the spine is glued or lined. For an adhesive-bound book, attach the ribbon before the first coat of PVA. In both cases the ribbon must be in place while the spine is still flexible enough to receive it.
Glue the ribbon into the spine head

Apply a thin coat of PVA to the head 20 mm of the spine surface. Lay the ribbon centrally along the spine, the cut end aligned with the head edge, the long tail pointing down toward the tail of the book. Press firmly with a bone folder. The ribbon sits in the natural groove between signatures. If fitting two ribbons, offset them side-by-side so they do not overlap. Allow to dry for 10 minutes, then proceed with spine lining.

The spine liner (mull or a strip of kraft) goes over the glued ribbon. Press the mull firmly over the full spine length. The ribbon passes under the mull at the head and emerges free at the tail. When the case is attached, the ribbon exits cleanly from under the headband. Trim the tail end of the ribbon to a neat angled cut. Seal the cut end with a small drop of PVA worked in with a fingertip.

items.symptom: The ribbon pulls free when the book is used
items.cause: Insufficient PVA at the head of the spine, or the mull was applied before the ribbon adhesive was tacky.
items.fix: Allow 10 minutes between gluing the ribbon and applying the mull. Check that the mull presses firmly over the ribbon at the head.

items.symptom: The ribbon frays at the tail end
items.cause: The cut end was not sealed.
items.fix: Apply a drop of PVA to the fraying end and press flat. For woven synthetic ribbons, a brief touch of a flame seals the end permanently.
